front: Figure: as a young man, threw incense at his father's funeral out of disfavor in the succession struggle, prompting a retainer's suicide
work: Early Life and Rise to Power

front: Figure: defeated his brother Nobuyuki at the Battle of Ino but pardoned him at their mother's urging
work: Early Life and Rise to Power

front: Figure: later faked illness to have his brother killed at Kiyosu Castle, using information from retainer Shibata Katsuie
work: Early Life and Rise to Power

front: Figure: eliminated a rival warlord by having an assassin lie in wait in the rival's own latrine and stab him with a spear
work: Early Life and Rise to Power

front: Figure: nearly killed when an Azai clan samurai charged his tent at the Battle of Anegawa
work: Early Life and Rise to Power

front: Battle: this man exploited a thunderstorm to defeat the much larger invading army of Imagawa Yoshimoto in a surprise attack
Battle: the victor allied with Matsudaira Motoyasu, the future Tokugawa Ieyasu, following this fight
Event: this man destroyed the Buddhist temple of Enryaku-ji, massacring its sohei warrior monks
work: Event: Siege of Mount Hiei and War on the Ikko-ikki

front: Event: this man besieged the Ikko-ikki fortress of Ishiyama Hongan-ji in Osaka for about a decade before forcing its surrender
work: Event: Siege of Mount Hiei and War on the Ikko-ikki

front: Event: this man destroyed an Ikko-ikki stronghold at Nagashima as revenge
work: Event: Siege of Mount Hiei and War on the Ikko-ikki

front: Battle: this man was defeated by Uesugi Kenshin here while attempting to seize Echigo province
work: Event: Siege of Mount Hiei and War on the Ikko-ikki

front: Battle: gunmen were arranged behind wooden palisades in three rotating ranks so volleys were never delayed by reloading, crushing the Takeda clan's cavalry
Battle: imported Portuguese arquebuses (tanegashima guns) were used here to repel the Takeda clan's cavalry
Battle: the victor allied with Tokugawa Ieyasu here after earlier turning against the last Ashikaga shogun, Ashikaga Yoshiaki
Ruler: broke guild monopolies and liberalized commerce through his rakuichi rakuza ("free markets, free guilds") policy
Ruler: allowed construction of the Nanban ("southern barbarian") temple in Kyoto
Ruler: after a siege that captured a mountaintop castle, renamed the castle, its village, and the province all "Gifu"
Ruler: built the first hirayamajiro ("flatland-mountain castle"), which became his base at Azuchi
Ruler: installed Ashikaga Yoshiaki as a puppet shogun before deposing him in 1573, ending the Ashikaga shogunate
Ruler: used a personal seal/motto reading "tenka fubu," translated as "all the world by force of arms" or "under the sky"
Figure: though an atheist, helped establish the first Christian church in Japan
Figure: obtained a black slave from Jesuit missionaries, whom he named Yasuke
Figure: after besieging the castles of Odani and Ichijodani, reportedly had some enemies' skulls made into cups for sake
Event: this man was betrayed and attacked by his own general Akechi Mitsuhide at Honno-ji temple in Kyoto, forcing him to commit seppuku
Event: this man's son also died alongside him in the same attack
Figure: was avenged and succeeded as unifier of Japan by his general Toyotomi Hideyoshi
Event: a Japanese poem holds that this man "pounds the rice [cake]," Hideyoshi "bakes the cake," and Tokugawa Ieyasu "eats it," summarizing the three unifiers' roles
